summary: We moved to Oakland in the summer of 1903 after living in Calistoga. Mother bought a house at 531 - 20th Street, and we settled down there while all the boys started working. I worked various jobs in Oakland, including at the Oakland Cream Depot and later commuting to San Francisco. Oakland was growing, with places like Temescal, Fruitvale, and Dimond nearby, and it had a lively community with stores, parks, and transportation hubs like the Oakland Mole.

Before moving to Oakland, we lived in Calistoga where my father managed the Rochdale store. Everett worked with him there, keeping books and clerking, and ran the store after Father died in 1902 until a new manager was hired. Ralph and I helped out by picking prunes and grapes during school vacations, and Mother took in summer boarders to help support us. It was a tough time but we managed with help from the Rochdale directors and others.

My father was the manager of the Rochdale store in Calistoga before he died in January 1902. Everett worked there with him and took over running the store until a new manager was appointed. Later, Everett worked for the Rochdale Wholesale Co. in San Francisco and continued to buy food from the Rochdale store for the family. The directors of the Rochdale store helped us out during hard times after Father's death.

I worked in San Francisco starting around June 1905 at Baker & Hamilton, crossing the bay daily from Oakland. The store was at Pine and Davis and Market Streets, a large four-story building with many departments. I also visited the Tivoli Theatre in San Francisco to see 'Floradora' and became familiar with the city’s lively streets and ferry landings. San Francisco was a bustling place with cobblestone streets and noisy drays, quite different from Oakland where I lived.
cues: Pine and Davis and Market Streets

summary: Ralph and I started attending Emerson School at 10th and Alice in Oakland, but only for a few days because we got jobs and had to work. School was short-lived for us as we needed to contribute to the family income. It was part of our early life in Oakland after we moved there in 1903.

I started working at Baker & Hamilton in San Francisco around June 1905, earning $15 a month. It was a well-established firm with a large four-story store where I learned about hardware and tools. My first job was as a weight clerk, and I worked my way through various departments. Everett helped me get the job, and it was an important step in my working life.

One day, Jack Schreiner, who lived next door, took me to the Tivoli Theatre in San Francisco to see 'Floradora.' It was the first show of that kind I ever saw, and it left a strong impression on me. The Tivoli was a notable place for entertainment in the city during those years.

I saw 'Floradora' at the Tivoli Theatre in San Francisco, thanks to Jack Schreiner who took me there. It was the first show of that kind I had ever seen, and it was quite memorable. This experience was part of my early exposure to city life and entertainment.

The Key System in Oakland wasn't built until several years after I started working in San Francisco. Back then, people crossed the bay on ferries, and when the boats arrived at the Oakland Mole, there was a bustling scene with hotel runners and hack drivers competing for fares. The Key System came later and changed how people traveled around town.

The Oakland Mole was the ferry landing where crowds came off boats connecting with trains. It was a lively place with hotel runners and hack drivers competing for passengers, sometimes so fiercely that a cop had to keep order. I crossed the bay from Oakland Mole to San Francisco daily when I worked at Baker & Hamilton.

Henry, the nail slinger at Baker & Hamilton, was a little guy from the Bowery. He was incredibly strong and fast, throwing 106-pound kegs of nails all day. He took a liking to me and even started teaching me to box. The Bowery was known as a tough place, and Henry embodied that spirit.

We had some celebrities in Oakland like Jack London. I remember reading his stories, including 'Tales of the Fish Patrol,' and I recall his yacht, the Snark, moored at the foot of Broadway before he sailed to the South Pacific. Once, while delivering a suit for M.J. Keller, I went to his place at 30th and Grove, though I don't remember if I met him then.

I read Jack London's 'Tales of the Fish Patrol,' which had been published in Youth's Companion. It was one of the stories that made him famous around the time we lived in Oakland. These stories were part of the local culture and excitement about his adventures.

I remember Jack London's yacht, the Snark, which was moored at the foot of Broadway in Oakland before he set off on his cruise to the South Pacific. Everett, Ralph, and I went to see it, and it was quite an event for us. The Snark was part of the local lore connected to London's adventurous life.

I remember seeing Joaquin Miller, whose real name was Cincinnatus Heine Miller, on Washington Street near Keller's store. He had long hair, a big felt hat, and boots he wore all the time. Years later, I went with some young men from the First Methodist Church to visit him on Thanksgiving morning, and even found him in bed but ready to greet us.

I was part of a group from the First Methodist Church that had a tradition of a short meeting and breakfast on Thanksgiving morning, followed by a hike. One year, we went to visit Joaquin Miller at his place, which was a memorable outing. The church was a part of our community life in Oakland.

Borax Smith made his fortune in the borax mines, specifically with Twenty Mule Team Borax, before moving to Oakland. He became involved in the streetcar and real estate business here. Jack London's novel 'Burning Daylight' was partly based on his life, which made him a notable figure in Oakland's history.

Twenty Mule Team Borax was the borax mining enterprise that made Borax Smith wealthy. It was famous enough that Jack London based part of his novel 'Burning Daylight' on Borax Smith's life. The borax business was an important part of California's industrial history connected to Oakland.

Jack London's novel 'Burning Daylight' was partly inspired by the life of Borax Smith, who was a prominent figure in Oakland. The story connected the borax mining business with the local history I knew about. It was interesting to see how real people influenced literature in our area.

Ina Coolbrith was the librarian and also served as the California state poet laureate. She was part of the rich cultural history of the area. Though I don't know the exact dates, her presence added to the literary heritage of Oakland and California.

Ina Coolbrith was the California poet laureate during the time I knew of her, and she was also the librarian. This title and role were important in promoting literature and culture in California, including Oakland. It was part of the state's recognition of its literary figures.

Robert Louis Stevenson lived in the area for a while, adding to the literary significance of Oakland and California. Though I don't know the exact dates, his presence was part of the rich history I was aware of growing up here. It was remarkable to have such famous writers connected to our region.

Temescal was a small town near Oakland when we arrived, located out at Telegraph Avenue and 50th Street. It wasn't much of a town, mostly Italian truck gardens and dairies, with some stores and a hotel. I remember going out there in the butter wagon and having lunch at an Italian place.

Fruitvale was one of the small towns east of Oakland when we moved here. It was part of the surrounding area that was mostly agricultural with dairies and gardens. It was a familiar name and place as I grew up in Oakland.

Dimond was another small town near Oakland, part of the agricultural landscape with dairies and truck gardens. It was close to where we lived and worked, and I remember it as part of the area's geography. These small towns were distinct but connected to Oakland's growth.

Allendale was one of the small communities around Oakland when we came here. Like the others, it was mostly rural with farms and dairies. It was part of the patchwork of neighborhoods that made up the greater Oakland area.

Melrose was another small town near Oakland, part of the agricultural and residential areas around the city. It was one of the places I knew by name as part of the Oakland region. These neighborhoods were quieter and more rural compared to the city center.

Elmhurst was one of the small towns or neighborhoods around Oakland when we moved here. It was part of the surrounding area with farms and dairies, contributing to the local economy. I remember it as part of the greater Oakland community.

San Leandro was another town near Oakland that I knew of. It was part of the East Bay area and shared similar agricultural and residential characteristics with the small towns around Oakland. It was familiar as part of the region's geography.

Idora Park was a local amusement park in the Oakland area, part of the community's recreational life. Though I didn't mention specific visits, it was known in the area and part of the social fabric. Places like Idora Park added to the character of Oakland in those days.
